title: LangGraph in Production: The WitUS Triage Agent
description: Build a real production LangGraph agent, one small piece at a time. You start by learning when a linear chain has outgrown itself and you actually need a graph, then design a clean state contract, wire typed tools, and add a durable human-in-the-loop approval gate with interrupt() and a Postgres checkpointer. The second module crashes the process on purpose to prove the gate survives a restart, and shows the two rules that keep your own interrupts correct. Grounded in the open-source WitUS Triage Agent code, TypeScript first with a Python durability track. Written to code along.

lessons.title: From a chain to a graph

questions.prompt: You are deciding between a chain and a LangGraph graph. Which situation is a genuine sign you have outgrown a chain?
options: A single prompt needs a longer system message
The program must pause for a human and resume later in a different process
You want to call a faster model
The output should be JSON instead of plain text
questions.explanation: Pausing and resuming across process boundaries is one of the three signs a chain has outgrown itself, because a chain's invoke() runs start to finish with no seam to stop at. A longer system message, a faster model, and JSON output are all still fine inside a plain chain.

questions.prompt: In the triage graph, what does the interrupt() call in the human_approval node need in order to survive a server restart?
options: A checkpointer that persists the paused state, keyed by thread_id
An HTTP request held open until the human responds
A retry loop that re-runs the whole graph
A longer function timeout
questions.explanation: interrupt() only suspends the graph; a checkpointer (here PostgresSaver) writes the paused state to Postgres under a thread_id so a later request can resume it. You cannot hold an HTTP request open for hours or across a deploy, re-running the whole graph would repeat completed work, and a longer timeout does not persist anything.

questions.prompt: What makes a LangGraph state field 'honest' in the sense used in the state-design lesson?
options: It has a default value
It is stored in the database
Some node genuinely produces it and some node genuinely consumes it
It is marked readonly
questions.explanation: An honest field is earned: a node writes it and a node reads it. Fields kept 'just in case' turn the state into a junk drawer. A default value, database storage, or a readonly marker say nothing about whether the field is actually load-bearing.

questions.prompt: Why does the project derive the Classification TypeScript type from a Zod schema with z.infer instead of writing the type separately?
options: z.infer makes the model respond faster
So the compile-time type, the runtime validation, and the model instruction all come from one definition and cannot drift apart
Because TypeScript cannot express enums
To avoid importing Zod at runtime
questions.explanation: The shape exists as a compile-time type, a runtime validator, and the structured-output instruction to the model. Deriving the type from one Zod schema means all three stay in sync; a hand-written duplicate type can silently drift. z.infer has no effect on model speed, TypeScript handles enums fine, and Zod is still used at runtime for validation.

questions.prompt: A run completes with category 'other' and confidence 0, and the test suite passes, but the classification is clearly wrong. What is the fastest way to find the real cause?
options: Add more assertions to the test
Open the LangSmith trace and read the classify node's model-call span
Rerun the graph several times
Increase the model temperature
questions.explanation: The trace shows the model-call span inside the classify node, where a failed call (for example an unfunded API key) was caught by the fail-soft fallback and never reached the logs. Tests that only assert a valid category cannot see a fallback firing, reruns repeat the same hidden failure, and temperature is unrelated.

questions.prompt: Why must a node that calls interrupt() be idempotent and contain no side effects before the interrupt?
options: Because LangGraph runs every node twice by default
Because on resume the node re-runs from its first line, so anything before interrupt() fires a second time
Because interrupts disable the checkpointer
Because Zod validation runs twice
questions.explanation: On resume LangGraph re-enters the interrupt node and runs it from the top, so a database write or notification placed before interrupt() would fire on both passes. Only interrupt nodes re-run this way, not every node; interrupts do not disable the checkpointer; and the doubling is about node re-execution, not Zod.

lessons.title: The crash test: in-memory state dies on restart
The ten-line fix: the Postgres checkpointer

questions.prompt: Under the default MemorySaver, a process pauses at interrupt() and then exits. A fresh process tries to resume the same thread and prints 'STATE LOST'. Why?
options: MemorySaver keeps checkpoints in the first process's heap, which is gone after it exits
The thread_id was mistyped
interrupt() deletes the checkpoint when it pauses
Postgres was not running
questions.explanation: MemorySaver stores every checkpoint in memory, so exiting the process erases the paused thread. The thread_id matched in the demo, interrupt() saves state rather than deleting it, and Postgres is not involved yet under MemorySaver.

questions.prompt: What actually changes when you switch from MemorySaver to PostgresSaver to make the agent durable?
options: The nodes and edges are rewritten to be stateless
Only the checkpointer passed at compile time changes; the graph itself is untouched
The model is swapped for a deterministic one
Every node must add a database write
questions.explanation: Durability is a compile-time swap: build a pool, construct PostgresSaver, call setup() once, and compile the same graph with it. The nodes, edges, and interrupt() are unchanged; no model swap or per-node database writes are required.

questions.prompt: You need a value to be available after the interrupt resumes. Where must it live?
options: In a module-level global variable
In a local variable inside the node
Returned into graph state before the interrupt
In an environment variable
questions.explanation: Only fields returned into graph state are written to the checkpoint and therefore survive a restart. Local variables and module globals live in the dead process's memory, and environment variables are not per-run state.

questions.prompt: Where should an irreversible side effect like charging a card go in a human-in-the-loop graph?
options: In the interrupt node, before interrupt() is called
In the interrupt node, after interrupt() returns
In a node after the approval gate, such as execute
In the conditional edge function
questions.explanation: The interrupt node re-runs from its first line on resume, so any side effect inside it risks firing twice. Irreversible work belongs in a node after the gate (execute or log_rejection), which runs exactly once. Edge functions are pure routers and must not have side effects.

questions.prompt: In LangSmith, what confirms that a resumed run continued correctly rather than replaying from the start?
options: The resumed run enters the graph mid-way, at human_approval, not at intake
The resumed run has a new thread_id
The checkpoints table is empty
The run took longer than the first one
questions.explanation: A correct resume loads the saved checkpoint and enters mid-graph at human_approval into finalize, not at intake. A new thread_id would mean it was not the same thread, an empty checkpoints table would mean nothing persisted, and run duration proves nothing about where it resumed.
